perturbation.original_text: 1.17 "Commercially Reasonable Efforts" means: (a) with respect to Sanofi, [***], consistent with [***] that [***], taking into account [***], including [***] and (b) with respect to RevMed, [***], consistent with [***] that [***], taking into account [***], including [***].
perturbation.changed_text: 1.17 "Commercially Reasonable Efforts" or "Best Efforts" means: (a) with respect to Sanofi, [***], consistent with [***] that [***], taking into account [***], including [***] and (b) with respect to RevMed, [***], consistent with [***] that [***], taking into account [***], including [***].
perturbation.explanation: Adding the term "Best Efforts" as an equivalent of "Commercially Reasonable Efforts" introduces ambiguity, especially since "Best Efforts" is a more stringent standard. Section 5.7, which requires Sanofi to use “Commercially Reasonable Efforts,” now becomes uncertain in its enforceability. It's unclear whether Sanofi is obligated to meet the "Commercially Reasonable Efforts" standard or the higher "Best Efforts" standard, leading to a contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5.7 Diligence. Consistent with [***] or as otherwise agreed by the Parties, Sanofi shall use Commercially Reasonable Efforts [***] to file and seek approval for an MAA for at least one Product in all of such countries or, in the case of the Major Market Countries in the European Union, through the centralized European Union approval process. If Sanofi materially breaches its obligation set forth in this Section 5.7, [***].
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces 'Best Efforts' as an equivalent to 'Commercially Reasonable Efforts'. This creates a contradiction in Section 5.7 because 'Best Efforts' is a higher standard than 'Commercially Reasonable Efforts.' Section 5.7 states that Sanofi shall use 'Commercially Reasonable Efforts', but with the added definition, it's ambiguous whether Sanofi is required to use 'Commercially Reasonable Efforts' or the more stringent 'Best Efforts', leading to uncertainty about their obligation and creating an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 8.7 Co-Promotion of Products in the United States. (a) RevMed shall have the one-time exclusive right to elect to assume up to [***]% (but not less than [***]%) of the Detailing effort for all Products in the United States (such geography, the "Co-Promotion Territory"; such right, the "Co-Promotion Option"; such Products that are co- promoted by the Parties, the "Co-Promotion Product"); provided that (i) [***] and (ii) RevMed shall provide to Sanofi, at the time of RevMed's exercise of the Co-Promotion Option pursuant to Section 8.7(b), a plan demonstrating to Sanofi's reasonable satisfaction that RevMed has, or will have on a timely basis, the necessary resources in place sufficient to Detail the applicable Co-Promotion Products in a manner consistent with and within the timelines required under the applicable Commercialization Plan. RevMed shall be obligated to perform the activities set forth in such plan within the timelines provided therein.
perturbation.changed_text: 8.7 Co-Promotion of Products in the United States. (a) RevMed shall have the one-time exclusive right to elect to assume up to [***]% (but not less than [***]%) of the Detailing effort for all Products in the United States (such geography, the "Co-Promotion Territory"; such right, the "Co-Promotion Option"; such Products that are co- promoted by the Parties, the "Co-Marketing Product"); provided that (i) [***] and (ii) RevMed shall provide to Sanofi, at the time of RevMed's exercise of the Co-Promotion Option pursuant to Section 8.7(b), a plan demonstrating to Sanofi's reasonable satisfaction that RevMed has, or will have on a timely basis, the necessary resources in place sufficient to Detail the applicable Co-Marketing Products in a manner consistent with and within the timelines required under the applicable Commercialization Plan. RevMed shall be obligated to perform the activities set forth in such plan within the timelines provided therein.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "Co-Promotion Product" to "Co-Marketing Product" without updating subsequent references creates contradiction. The contract now inconsistently refers to the same concept using different terms. This introduces uncertainty into the legal agreement, since the meaning and scope of obligations associated with both terms become undefined and subject to multiple interpretations. In Section 8.7(b), it now references "Co-Promotion Option" which makes sense, and "Co-Marketing Product" which create the in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: (b) Sanofi shall notify RevMed of the anticipated launch date for the first Product in the Co-Promotion Territory at least [***] in advance thereof. If RevMed wishes to exercise its one-time Co-Promotion Option, it shall so notify Sanofi in writing at least [***] prior to the anticipated launch of such Product in the Co-Promotion Territory. If (i) RevMed does not provide the above election notice in compliance with the requirements of this Section 8.7(b), or (ii) RevMed provides notice to Sanofi that it does not intend to exercise its one-time Co-Promotion Option, then RevMed shall be deemed to have waived such one-time right to co-promote any and all Products in the Co-Promotion Territory. For clarity, once RevMed has exercised its Co- Promotion Option pursuant to this Section 8.7(b), RevMed's right to co-promote Products shall apply to all other existing and subsequent Products in the Co-Promotion Territory.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces "Co-Promotion Product" with "Co-Marketing Product" in Section 8.7(a). However, the contradicted text in Section 8.7(b) still uses the term "Co-Promotion Option" and implicitly refers to "Co-Promotion Product", creating an in-text contradiction. The contract now inconsistently uses both "Co-Marketing Product" and the original "Co-Promotion Product", leading to ambiguity and uncertainty about which term applies in which context, thus creating a strong in-text contradiction as it falls under the definition of Misaligned Terminology.

perturbation.original_text: 12.2 Termination. (a) Terminations by Sanofi. (i) Termination by Sanofi for Convenience. Sanofi may terminate this Agreement (A) in its entirety by providing [***] written notice of termination to RevMed or (B) on a country-by-country or Product-by-Product basis by providing [***] written notice of termination to RevMed; provided that if Sanofi desires to terminate this Agreement under this Section 12.2(a)(i)B only with respect to the U.S. (for all Products or one or more Products), Sanofi shall provide [***] written notice of termination to RevMed.
perturbation.changed_text: 12.2 Termination. (a) Terminations by Sanofi. (i) Termination by Sanofi for Convenience. Sanofi may terminate this Agreement (A) in its entirety by providing [***] written notice of termination to RevMed or (B) on a country-by-country or Item-by-Item basis by providing [***] written notice of termination to RevMed; provided that if Sanofi desires to terminate this Agreement under this Section 12.2(a)(i)B only with respect to the U.S. (for all Items or one or more Items), Sanofi shall provide [***] written notice of termination to RevMed.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing "Product-by-Product" with "Item-by-Item" without updating subsequent instances creates confusion. The term "Product" has a clearly defined meaning. "Item" is undefined and could refer to anything, leading to uncertainty. For instance, does terminating "Item-by-Item" allow Sanofi to terminate specific components of a product, which conflicts with selling of "Product" with the intended meaning. This contradicts and makes ambiguous the specific instances where the word "Product" is being used later on, because with the addition of Item, it is unlcear if it is also reffering to Product as intended, or "Item".
perturbation.contradicted_text: (iii) For Safety. Sanofi will have the right to terminate this Agreement in its entirety or on a country-by-country or Product-by-Product basis, upon [***] prior written notice to RevMed, due to safety concerns raised by a Regulatory Authority, an Institutional Review Board for a Clinical Trial or by Sanofi's internal regulatory decision makers acting in accordance with Sanofi's standard internal policies (any such entity or group, a "Safety Reviewer"), where such Safety Reviewer recommends cessation of Development or Commercialization of such SHP2 Inhibitor or Product with respect to any SHP2 Inhibitor or Product (and a summary of such concerns will be stated in the notice of termination). During such [***] notice period, each Party will continue to perform all of its obligations under this Agreement then in effect.
perturbation.justification: The change from "Product-by-Product" to "Item-by-Item" introduces ambiguity and potential conflict. The original text consistently uses "Product" with a presumably defined scope, whereas "Item" is undefined and could include components or aspects of a product. The contradicted text, referring to termination based on safety concerns related to a "Product", relies on a clear and consistent definition of 'Product.' By introducing "Item", it's now unclear whether a safety concern related to a specific component (an 'Item') would trigger the same termination rights as a safety concern for the entire 'Product'. This misalignment of terminology leads to an in-text contradiction as the definition of Product becomes ambiguous in the context of termination rights and the definition of an Item is unknown, creating uncertainty regarding obligations and is thus, a strong contradiction.
